chapters.title: — Headline Setup: Who's in the Room
— Headline Deep Dive: Effect Size and What It Means
— Round 1: Proteinuria Severity and Long-Term Cardiorenal Risk (BJOG)
— Round 3: 22-Week Survival and the Intervention Bundle (AJOG MFM)
— Round 4: FMF Calibration Across Conception Modes (Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol)

follow_up_links.reason: Provides RCT-level evidence that biomarker-driven first-trimester screening and aspirin prophylaxis delay preterm preeclampsia in chronic hypertensives—directly informing whether to screen universally or treat empirically.
follow_up_links.title: First-trimester prediction and prevention of preterm pre-eclampsia in women with chronic hypertension.

follow_up_links.reason: First large propensity-matched EMR analysis linking ongoing semaglutide use into pregnancy with adverse outcomes, sharpening preconception counseling for patients on GLP-1 agonists.
follow_up_links.title: Gestational Weight Gain and Pregnancy Outcomes After Semaglutide Exposure.

follow_up_links.reason: Quantifies how proteinuria severity at preeclampsia diagnosis predicts decades-long maternal cardiorenal risk, enabling more individualized postpartum surveillance.
follow_up_links.title: Proteinuria in Preeclampsia and Long-Term Risk of Maternal Kidney and Cardiovascular Disease: A Population-Based Cohort Study.

follow_up_links.reason: Identifies specific interventions—steroids, ventilation, surfactant—that independently improve one-year survival at 22 weeks, strengthening evidence-based periviability counseling.
follow_up_links.title: Improving 1-Year Survival at 22 Weeks Gestation: Which Perinatal Interventions Matter?

follow_up_links.reason: Demonstrates that FMF preeclampsia screening calibration varies by ART versus spontaneous conception, prompting context-specific interpretation in fertility-treatment pregnancies.
follow_up_links.title: Predictive performance of Fetal Medicine Foundation first-trimester screening algorithm for pre-eclampsia according to conception mode.

key_claims.claim: In women with chronic hypertension, the FMF first-trimester competing-risks model accurately identifies those at elevated risk for preterm preeclampsia, and aspirin prophylaxis in screen-positive patients delays delivery due to preterm disease.

Continued semaglutide exposure into pregnancy is associated with abnormal gestational weight gain patterns and higher rates of adverse pregnancy outcomes compared with discontinuation before conception or no exposure.
Greater severity of proteinuria during a preeclamptic pregnancy independently predicts increased long-term maternal risk of chronic hypertension, chronic kidney disease, and cardiovascular disease.
Among 22-week live births managed with active intervention, antenatal corticosteroids, mechanical ventilation, and surfactant administration each independently improve one-year survival.

Performance of the FMF first-trimester preeclampsia screening algorithm differs by conception mode, with varying preeclampsia incidence across ART, ovulation-induction, and spontaneous conception groups.
